The AI infrastructure boom is real and accelerating fast—McKinsey's $5.2T baseline capex projection by 2030 (with IT equipment and power as the big drivers) matches the explosive demand we're seeing for compute at scale. 

Betting on the "picks and shovels" like photonics, CPUs, memory, and especially power gen is the high-conviction play here. The buildout won't pause; it's the foundation for everything next. Smart call.

text:
Light Speed: Why Photonics Is the Invisible Backbone of AI

KEY HIGHLIGHT

- The Marvell $MRVL + Inphi angle is the one most people underestimate. DSP chips are to optical transceivers what the engine is to a car — you cannot ship a high-speed optical module without one, and Marvell owns that market position. Every 800G and 1.6T transceiver deployed in an AI cluster is a Marvell DSP royalty.

- Coherent $COHR is the most comprehensive pure-play but also the most complex story — the II-VI/Finisar merger created integration challenges and a leveraged balance sheet. The thesis is right but execution risk is real. Worth sizing accordingly.

- Fabrinet $FN is the sleeper that rarely gets discussed. They are the contract manufacturer for most of the photonics industry, which means they get volume leverage without technology concentration risk. When the whole industry is growing, Fabrinet grows with all of it.

- Credo $CRDO is the highest-beta, most speculative name on the list — but also the one with the most potential to surprise to the upside if the AI networking upgrade cycle accelerates faster than expected.

- And the private company to watch most closely is Ayar Labs — if optical chiplets become the standard interface for next-gen AI processors, whoever owns that technology gets acquired at a price that will seem obvious in retrospect.

text:
Yes, that's exactly what aleabitoreddit is saying.

Photonics is in a full supercycle (started ~H1/H2 2025). Some leaders like $LITE & $COHR already ran 500-1000% so you're a bit late there (though they see 50%+ more upside possible). 

You're extremely early on next-gen architectures (CPO, quantum dot, etc.) and new pure plays. Buy the dips in those emerging names—they're calling it the new gold mine for retail before institutions pile in.

as promised, here is more on photonics 

1 with buy signals, 7 with sell signals

Sector running hot. Signals have to scream Sell due to overextended. But a 'Sell' signal doesn't always mean a crash; sometimes it just means a rest stop. It’s possible the engines are just cooling down before the next leg up. The trend isn't broken … til proven otherwise

$MRVL , $AAOI , $AEHR , $LPTH, $LWLG , $UMC , $NOK , 
$SIVEF

System is layered with 4 buy and sell signals using different methods to ensure no move is missed, and also to show the confidence of the correct move.

HI CERTAINTY SELL:
any shape on top of candle (♦️, 🟪, ◼️, regardless of color)= sell

HI CERTAINLY BUY:
any shape on bottom of candle (🟩 , 🔹, ◼️, regardless of color) = buy

MEDIUM RISK BUY:
. green star under candle. Mostly accurate. Java haven’t been able to code my brain - to invalidate green star under certain conditions. I will have to manually call those green stars until Java gets it.

. ‘B’ under a candle: has capitulated, tentatively confirmed to be trending up, yet might still be invalidated later. Keep an eye on it if did buy

EARLY WARNING/EARLY WATCH:
⭐️ white star on top of candle = watch for potential sell signal

⭐️ white star on bottom of candle = watch for potential buy signal. Do not buy on 1st, 2nd stars. 3rd star could be a buy, but better to wait for high certainty buy signals

EXPLANING THE FORCE BEHIND THE MOVE UP:
⬇️ bright green arrow down on top = Smart Money support

 CHAOS ALERT:
⬇️⬇️⬇️ cluster of white arrows = big move coming, up or down

TREND DIRECTION:
🟦 blue candle = uptrend
🟨 yellow = downtrend 

BAND:
📈 above midline = bullish 
📉 below midline = bearish

text:
Which earnings are you looking forward to most this week?

We’re getting major updates across several key trends:
1. Photonics/Networks: $GLW, $CLS, $APH
2. Memory: $STX, $SNDK, $WDC
3. Grid/Energy: $BE, $XOM, $CVX
4. Finance: $HOOD, $SOFI
5. Magnificent 7: $META, $AMZN, $GOOGL, $MSFT, $AAPL
6. AI Software: $ZETA, $LMND

Very exciting week ahead. 

If Trump doesn’t do anything out of the box, it looks like we’re in for a really strong week for the S&P 500.

I’ll be paying especially close attention to sentiment in the statements, execution, forward guidance, and industry insights - particularly in Memory, Photonics, and Energy. 

My top focus: $SNDK, $GLW, and $BE.

Some analysts are calling for 40% revenue growth at $ZETA - really curious to see if they deliver.

On $HOOD and $SOFI, I’m not expecting extraordinary results. Robinhood in particular remains heavily tied to crypto market volatility and success which has recently been low.

As for the Mag 7, five of them report this week - crazy!

I’ll be watching cloud revenue growth closely at $AMZN, $GOOG, and $MSFT. 

For $META, I want updates on AI applications across their business segments, AI project progress, and ad spend trends. 

For $AAPL, I’m looking for order numbers on their recent product launches plus any fresh details on AI projects and integrations.

text:
Amkor $AMKR is currently playing out a similar pattern to many other photonics/optics/AI supply chain companies; the Bull Flag breakout!

After months of consolidation we’ve seen a huge breakout over the last few weeks, but where do we go from here?

$AMKR is currently up about 98% YTD and is looking stretched now on almost all timeframes.

Short term; $AMKR likely needs to consolidate for a few weeks, to cool down, allow the moving averages to catch up, for institutions to accumulate more shares and to build momentum for another move up.

Fundamentally; $AMKR is the go-to advanced packaging supplier for both $AVGO and $TSMC - if Amkor can continue scaling production, expect this to continue to feed through into revenues (and profits) and further long term growth.

If $AMKR can continue to execute, their stock will continue to climb long term.

text: Huge beat on $QCOM could be signaling the bottom of the Smartphone Market, where it makes up a third of $GFS Revenue, could explain part of the surge in Volume seen on their OBV chart, instead of just a bet on the Photonics market; $GFS OBV has once again finished above it's Dec'22 ATH level, if it continues holding above past earnings, this stock will see ATHs again soon.
embedded_text:
If $QCOM sees a bottom in smartphone, it means there is also a bottom for $SOI and $GFS's core business.

If these see the weakest part of their business rebound while the bull thesis relies on photonics, there's no limit to what I'll tell my boss in a few months. https://t.co/KGNaFypf4D

For the first time, researchers at QuiX Quantum have demonstrated below threshold error mitigation in a photonic quantum computer. They used a technique called photon distillation, where multiple imperfect photons are combined through quantum interference to produce a cleaner, more identical photon. On a 20-mode photonic processor, this method reduced photon indistinguishability errors by 2.2× and achieved a 1.2× net reduction in total system error. That matters because quantum systems usually introduce more errors than they fix. This flips that balance. It could significantly reduce the number of photon sources needed, potentially by up to 4×, making photonic quantum computers far more practical 👀 In other words, this approach improves qubit quality at the source, bringing scalable, light based quantum computing much closer to reality."}}}, "rest_id": "2049521863710405103", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1318", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2049623614115246266", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "28", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
CPO, SILICON PHOTONICS, AND OPTICAL INTERCONNECT

CPO IS MOVING FROM R&D TO EARLY PRODUCTION, BUT THE NEAR-TERM DOLLARS REMAIN SMALL RELATIVE TO THE STRATEGIC SIGNAL (READ-THROUGH 10)

Call support: Management said FormFactor is “now beginning to ramp our TRITON production test system, co-developed with Advantest and Tokyo Electron,” and that the ramp is accelerating. Management attributed the acceleration to “growing volumes of CPO chips planned for later this year” and FormFactor’s leadership in Test Insertion 1, which ensures known-good die on the photonic integrated circuit wafer. Management also cautioned that CPO remains in “the very early innings” and that other insertions are “proving to be difficult for customers to implement in production.”

Affected companies: Advantest Corporation (6857: Japan) and Tokyo Electron Ltd. (8035: Japan) are positively affected with low near-term magnitude and higher long-duration optionality. The transmission mechanism is direct: TRITON was co-developed with these partners, and early CPO production test creates future attach opportunities for ATE, probers, and co-developed test platforms.

Broadcom Inc. (AVGO: US), Marvell Technology Inc. (MRVL: US), Coherent Corp. (COHR: US), Lumentum Holdings Inc. (LITE: US), and Fabrinet (FN: US) are positively affected with low near-term but potentially meaningful long-duration magnitude. The mechanism is validation of silicon photonics and CPO moving toward production use cases in AI networking. If CPO becomes necessary to address bandwidth density and power constraints, optical component suppliers, silicon photonics developers, and AI networking ASIC vendors benefit from a larger production ecosystem.

Teradyne Inc. (TER: US) is mixed to modestly positive. Management emphasized an open ecosystem and specifically said FormFactor works closely with Teradyne, but the named TRITON co-development partners were Advantest and Tokyo Electron. The mechanism is that Teradyne can still participate in future CPO insertions, but the earliest named production-test system appears more closely tied to Advantest and Tokyo Electron.

Near-term trading catalyst: positive for CPO sentiment but limited in revenue magnitude. The call supports the view that CPO is no longer purely a lab topic, but it does not indicate that CPO is yet a large 2026 revenue category for the broader supply chain.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: potentially high. The critical point is that Insertion 1 is focused on validating known-good photonic integrated circuits before expensive downstream packaging steps. If this becomes a standard production insertion, optical probing and electro-optical test can become recurring infrastructure layers in AI networking manufacturing.

DOWNSTREAM HARDWARE AND OEMS

AI SUPPLY-CHAIN TIGHTNESS IS POSITIVE FOR SEMICONDUCTOR VENDORS BUT NEGATIVE FOR HARDWARE OEM MARGIN FLEXIBILITY (READ-THROUGH 11)

Call support: Management described DRAM as supply constrained, HBM demand stepping up, networking demand strong, data-center CPU probe demand improving, CPO chip volumes planned later in 2026, and probe-card capacity constraints across FormFactor and competitors. The call also noted isolated cases where customers are willing to pay expedite fees in a capacity-constrained environment.

Affected companies: Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL: US), Hewlett Packard Enterprise Co. (HPE: US), Super Micro Computer Inc. (SMCI: US), Lenovo Group Ltd. (0992: Hong Kong), and HP Inc. (HPQ: US) are negatively affected with low-to-moderate magnitude on margin and delivery-risk grounds, while remaining positively exposed to AI server demand. The transmission mechanism is input-cost and availability pressure. If DRAM, HBM, probe cards, and advanced-packaging capacity remain tight, OEMs and server integrators face higher component costs, longer lead times, and reduced ability to optimize gross margin.

Apple Inc. (AAPL: US) is modestly negatively affected through memory availability and potential LPDDR/DRAM cost pressure. The mechanism is less directly AI-server related but still relevant if DRAM wafer starts are dynamically allocated across HBM, DDR5, DDR4, and other designs to maximize supplier margin.

Near-term trading catalyst: negative for gross-margin assumptions at hardware OEMs if memory price increases and AI component shortages persist.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: mixed. AI infrastructure demand supports server revenue growth, but the profit pool may continue migrating upstream toward memory suppliers, advanced-packaging providers, foundries, and test suppliers rather than downstream assemblers and OEMs.

CONSUMER SEMICONDUCTOR RECOVERY IS NOT THE DRIVER; AI INFRASTRUCTURE IS ABSORBING THE INCREMENTAL TEST AND CAPACITY DOLLARS (READ-THROUGH 12)

Call support: Management described Q2 Foundry & Logic growth as driven primarily by data-center CPU strength, with continued networking strength and “steady demand in PC and mobile.” The word “steady” is important because it contrasts with the stronger language used for HBM, networking, GPUs, custom ASICs, and CPO.

Affected companies: Qualcomm Inc. (QCOM: US), MediaTek Inc. (2454: Taiwan), Qorvo Inc. (QRVO: US), Skyworks Solutions Inc. (SWKS: US), and other mobile-exposed semiconductor suppliers are neutral to modestly negatively affected from a relative-growth perspective. The transmission mechanism is capital and capacity allocation. The call suggests incremental advanced test and probe demand is being pulled toward AI infrastructure rather than broad PC or mobile demand acceleration.

Apple Inc. (AAPL: US), HP Inc. (HPQ: US), and Lenovo Group Ltd. (0992: Hong Kong) are neutral to modestly negatively affected as end-market read-throughs. The mechanism is that upstream test demand is not indicating a strong consumer device rebound; PC and mobile are stable contributors, not the source of upside.

Near-term trading catalyst: negative for broad consumer semiconductor beta if investors are looking for evidence of a synchronized semiconductor recovery. The FormFactor call supports a narrower AI-led cycle.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: positive for AI infrastructure concentration and negative for the relative importance of consumer electronics in leading-edge semiconductor capital allocation. The call reinforces that the incremental complexity dollars are accruing to HBM, AI networking, CPU inference, GPU, custom ASIC, CPO, and advanced packaging rather than to traditional PC/mobile cycles.

text:
#SIVE $SIVE #5G #6G #Beamforming #FWA #SATCOM #CHIPSAct #Semiconductors #Wireless #Nokia

Take a moment to read this, friends — we’re going to talk about the unknown side of Sivers.

A lot of investors — and me first — only knew Sivers for its photonics business (the lasers for AI datacenters). 

It’s exciting and has massive potential. But after digging a bit deeper, we discover a real hidden gem: the Wireless division.

 It still accounts for ~70% of current revenue, delivered very strong growth (+85% on some quarters in 2025), and provides solid cash-flow stability.

 It’s the quiet but powerful pillar that makes the entire $SIVE investment thesis much less risky.

First of all, what is a beamformer? (simple explanation) : 

Imagine you want to send a super-fast internet signal (5G, future 6G, or satellite connection) to a phone, a house, or a satellite.

A regular radio signal spreads out in all directions, like a light bulb lighting up an entire room. It works, but it’s very inefficient. You waste a lot of energy, the signal weakens quickly over distance, and there are lots of interferences with other devices around.

A beamformer (literally “beam former”) is like an ultra-precise smart projector:
Instead of spreading everywhere, it concentrates all the signal power into a very narrow directional beam pointed exactly at your phone or your house.

Result? The connection is much faster, much more stable, reaches much farther… and uses way less energy.

Sivers Wireless develops exactly these mmWave beamformers (chips + antenna arrays). 
This technology is essential for high-speed 5G/6G, Fixed Wireless Access (FWA = wireless internet instead of fiber), SATCOM, and defense applications.

Major wins to date (April 2026):

• January 2025 → $5.4M contract (60 M SEK) signed with a global Tier-1 ( The crowd is murmering Nokia ? ) 

It’s for the next-generation beamforming transceivers. Even better: the collaboration is planned across multiple generations.

• New → Firm $3M purchase order with Tachyon Networks (28 GHz FWA). Production and shipments already starting in 2026 — real revenue incoming.

• 3 design wins in 5G/FWA already secured → potential of around 1 million units over the first three years of production (starting 2026).

• India → Historic partnership with Intel + WiSig Networks + office opened in Bangalore to target the massive FWA market.

• Defense → CHIPS Act funding (2nd round ongoing), contracts with Raytheon, BAE Systems, Ericsson. Daybreak™ 7-15 GHz ICs now available (March 2026) — very competitive in power and efficiency.

• SATCOM → Cloudchaser chipset + Maverick antenna arrays launched (Feb 2026) + strong commercial interest.

• 6G → Already involved in ISAC research with Nokia.

Why is this strategic for $SIVE?

Wireless brings stability and predictable revenues while the AI photonics division ramps into mass production (2026-2027). It’s the financial cushion that lets the company breathe… and it has strong potential to become a real cash cow from 2026-2028.

And even if photonics hits some delays or doesn’t deliver as expected, the Wireless division alone is strong enough to justify a major re-rating on its own.

In short: $SIVE is not just an AI laser story.

With the recent news of the Photonics spin-off to Silicon Valley, $SIVE (Sweden) is effectively becoming a pure-play leader in Wireless/SATCOM/Defense, while still holding the majority of the US AI laser powerhouse. It’s the ultimate value unlock.

It’s also a powerful play in mmWave 5G/6G, SATCOM, defense, and emerging markets — with technology that is already generating revenue.

I hope this clarified some things and/or brought new info for the Sivers community!

This is not financial advice. I’m just digging into this company and sharing what I found.

3238}, {"from_index": 3710, "richtext_types": ["Italic"], "to_index": 3800}]}, "text": "#SIVE $SIVE #5G #6G #Beamforming #FWA #SATCOM #CHIPSAct #Semiconductors #Wireless #Nokia Take a moment to read this, friends — we’re going to talk about the unknown side of Sivers. A lot of investors — and me first — only knew Sivers for its photonics business (the lasers for AI datacenters). It’s exciting and has massive potential. But after digging a bit deeper, we discover a real hidden gem: the Wireless division. It still accounts for ~70% of current revenue, delivered very strong growth (+85% on some quarters in 2025), and provides solid cash-flow stability. It’s the quiet but powerful pillar that makes the entire $SIVE investment thesis much less risky. First of all, what is a beamformer? (simple explanation) : Imagine you want to send a super-fast internet signal (5G, future 6G, or satellite connection) to a phone, a house, or a satellite. A regular radio signal spreads out in all directions, like a light bulb lighting up an entire room. It works, but it’s very inefficient. You waste a lot of energy, the signal weakens quickly over distance, and there are lots of interferences with other devices around. A beamformer (literally “beam former”) is like an ultra-precise smart projector: Instead of spreading everywhere, it concentrates all the signal power into a very narrow directional beam pointed exactly at your phone or your house. Result? The connection is much faster, much more stable, reaches much farther… and uses way less energy. Sivers Wireless develops exactly these mmWave beamformers (chips + antenna arrays). This technology is essential for high-speed 5G/6G, Fixed Wireless Access (FWA = wireless internet instead of fiber), SATCOM, and defense applications. Major wins to date (April 2026): • January 2025 → $5.4M contract (60 M SEK) signed with a global Tier-1 ( The crowd is murmering Nokia ? ) It’s for the next-generation beamforming transceivers. Even better: the collaboration is planned across multiple generations. • New → Firm $3M purchase order with Tachyon Networks (28 GHz FWA). Production and shipments already starting in 2026 — real revenue incoming. • 3 design wins in 5G/FWA already secured → potential of around 1 million units over the first three years of production (starting 2026). • India → Historic partnership with Intel + WiSig Networks + office opened in Bangalore to target the massive FWA market. • Defense → CHIPS Act funding (2nd round ongoing), contracts with Raytheon, BAE Systems, Ericsson. Daybreak™ 7-15 GHz ICs now available (March 2026) — very competitive in power and efficiency. • SATCOM → Cloudchaser chipset + Maverick antenna arrays launched (Feb 2026) + strong commercial interest. • 6G → Already involved in ISAC research with Nokia. Why is this strategic for $SIVE? Wireless brings stability and predictable revenues while the AI photonics division ramps into mass production (2026-2027). It’s the financial cushion that lets the company breathe… and it has strong potential to become a real cash cow from 2026-2028. And even if photonics hits some delays or doesn’t deliver as expected, the Wireless division alone is strong enough to justify a major re-rating on its own. In short: $SIVE is not just an AI laser story. With the recent news of the Photonics spin-off to Silicon Valley, $SIVE (Sweden) is effectively becoming a pure-play leader in Wireless/SATCOM/Defense, while still holding the majority of the US AI laser powerhouse. It’s the ultimate value unlock. It’s also a powerful play in mmWave 5G/6G, SATCOM, defense, and emerging markets — with technology that is already generating revenue. I hope this clarified some things and/or brought new info for the Sivers community! This is not financial advice. I’m just digging into this company and sharing what I found. #SIVE $SIVE #5G #6G #Beamforming #FWA #SATCOM #CHIPSAct #Semiconductors #Wireless #Nokia"}}}, "previous_counts": {"bookmark_count": 0, "favorite_count": 0, "quote_count": 0, "reply_count": 0, "retweet_count": 0}, "rest_id": "2049610156166570331", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "329", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text: Seeing $TSEM going Parabolic is what got me so obsessed with $GFS doing the same thing, until I started reading how GloFo's photonics aren't exactly up to par with Towers, and realized why the stock has been so badly shorted 😵 Anyway, they are starting to catch up, and $AMD is using $GFS for the their MI500 GPUs! If I'm right, GlobalFoundries will hit $100 by early November where my Finger Icon is pointing, when it intersects the 3/1 Gann Fan Angle from it's July'22 low, and the 1/1 Gann Fan Angle from it's April'25 low.   $GFS is shorted to the gills with 40% of the float short, the photonics bonanza could definitely send this sucker to $100 in frenzy, bookmarking this post in case it turns out as predicted.

user_name: ゆるっと半導体｜AI・半導体ニュースを毎朝やさしく解説
text:
光コンピュータって聞いたことある？ボクがいま注目してるのは、オックスフォード大学発祥のLumaiという企業なんだ。

ElectronicsじゃなくPhotonics（光）でAIチップの課題を解く。通常のGPUは電子で計算するけど、光は速度が違うんだよ。消費電力も少なくて済む。

実はこのアプローチ、業界内では「次の大波」って見られてるんだ。Lumaiだけじゃなく、新興企業が光演算にシフトしてるのは、NVIDIAも監視してるぐらい。

2026年いま、データセンターの電力危機が本当に深刻化してる。そこに光が答えになるかもしれないってのが、ベンチャーから大手まで考えてることなんだ。

→ 長期投資家は、TSMC製造の半導体だけじゃなく『素材から新しい技術体系』が育つ瞬間も追う価値があるよ。

text:
Let's look at the harsh realities of scaling AI clusters and why the transition to light is mandatory:
​🔴 Copper Interconnects: Overheat and lose severe signal integrity at extreme data rates (800G and 1.6T).
🟢 Silicon Photonics: Uses lasers to transfer data near the speed of light with a fraction of the power consumption.
​With hyperscalers burning billions on electricity, is moving data optically the only way to prevent a total grid collapse?
$ASML $AVGO #semiconductors #ai
(2/4)

text:
THE PHOTONICS ROTATION

Almost nobody is watching photonics.

As AI clusters scale, copper hits physical limits and the next bottleneck becomes optical infrastructure.

Here are 15 names positioned for it:

1. $LITE owns the laser + optical switching side of the trade and is one of the cleanest pure plays on AI optical demand.

2. $COHR wins from lasers, modules, and networking hardware that power hyperscale AI infrastructure and cloud expansion.

3. $AAOI is one of the best ways to play AI optical transceivers with major hyperscaler demand for 800G and 1.6T connectivity.

4. $SIVE benefits from the push toward faster semiconductor-to-optical integration as AI infrastructure scales.

5. $MRVL controls a huge part of the DSP + interconnect story with optical networking chips and high-speed connectivity.

6. $AVGO sits at the center of AI networking through switching, custom silicon, and optical interconnect demand.

7. $ANET is the Ethernet backbone moving massive AI workloads across hyperscale clusters and data centers.

8. $GLW supplies the specialty glass + fiber needed for the optical transport layer behind AI infrastructure.

9. $JBL benefits from building and scaling the actual hardware behind networking systems and optical modules.

10. $AEHR wins from burn-in + testing demand as AI ASICs and high-power optical hardware move into production.

11. $POET is focused on lower-cost optical engines designed to improve efficiency inside AI data centers.

12. $LWLG is pushing next-gen polymer photonics that could make optical communication faster and more efficient.

13. $QCLS brings exposure to advanced laser systems supporting precision photonics and next-gen optical demand.

14. $LPTH provides specialty optics and photonic components tied to industrial, defense, and AI-driven systems.

15. $ALAB gives exposure to the connectivity + infrastructure side helping AI clusters scale faster.

Most people won’t care until these are already up 100%.

text:
Three distinct approaches to quantum hardware are now trading as the sector's most active stocks by dollar volume: trapped-ion systems with major cloud integrations, quantum annealing with hybrid solvers, and integrated photonics running at room temperature.

This lineup reveals the sheer diversity of bets the market is placing. These aren't just competitors on the same track—they represent fundamentally different architectures, each with its own thesis on how quantum advantage arrives. Cloud-accessible ion traps, purpose-built optimization machines, and portable photonic devices barely share a vocabulary, let alone a roadmap.

That diversity is a sign of a maturing ecosystem. Capital spreading across competing paradigms shows the market is pricing in genuine technical uncertainty, which is the honest position to hold right now. Nobody has a monopoly on the path to broad commercial adoption.

The common thread is that all three are building real access points for enterprise users, whether through cloud marketplaces, developer toolkits, or turnkey hardware. Providers lowering the barrier to experimentation today are positioning themselves to capture demand when quantum workloads scale tomorrow #QuantumComputing

is the most comprehensive photonics stack there is. L1: Materials & Substrates The foundation. Without this, nothing above it exists. L1 companies grow the raw crystal wafers – InP, GaAs, Ge that silicon can’t replace for high-speed photonics. Key names: $AXTI, $SOI, $IQE, $AIXA, $COHR, $LASR Top performer: AXT (AXTI) +6,837% InP demand exploded for AI datacenter optical connectivity. Q3 2025 InP revenues up 250%+ sequentially. Doubling capacity by end of 2026. L2: Active Photonic Devices This is where light gets made. Lasers, modulators, photodetectors. In a CPO world, these get embedded directly into switch ASICs – no longer pluggable add-ons. That’s a structural demand shift, not a cycle. Key names: $LITE, $COHR, $LASR, $SIVEF, $AAOI, $MTSI, $SMTC Top performer: Lumentum (LITE) +1,685% Named NVIDIA collaborator on Spectrum-X silicon photonics. Recently received the largest single CPO laser purchase commitment in company history. L3: Electro-Optical Connectivity ICs The translators. Silicon that bridges optical and electrical worlds. TIAs, drivers, DSPs, SerDes, retimers. As speeds push to 800G and 1.6T, this layer becomes a chokepoint. Key names: $MTSI, $SMTC, $MRVL, $CRDO, $ALAB, $MXLV Top performer: Credo (CRDO) +361% Not a pure photonics play – but every 800G and 1.6T deployment runs through their AEC and SerDes IP. L4: PIC Platform & IP The brain of the photonic system. Waveguides, modulators, and photodetectors on a single chip. Highest-IP layer in the stack. Whoever locks platform relationships here wins the next decade. Key names: $POET, $MRVL, $CRDO, $IOLI, $EOPT (Ayar Labs, Lightmatte) Top performer: Innolight +948% Partnered with Tower Semi on a breakthrough SiPh platform that cuts lasers per module in half. L5: Foundry & Process Platform You need a fab. Not just any fab. SiPh fabrication needs specialized processes – TFLN, SiPh COUPE, SoIC – that only a handful of foundries can run globally. Key names: $TSEM, $GFS, $UMC, $TSM Top performer: Tower Semi (TSEM) +542% SiPh revenue hit ~$52M in Q3 2025 alone, up 70% YoY. Full-year 2025 target: $220M+. Q4 annualized run rate: $320M+. Investing $650M to triple SiPh shipments by mid-2026. L6A: Engine & Module Where the optical engine becomes a product – 400G, 800G, 1.6T modules. Most commoditized layer today. CPO transition will redraw it entirely. Key names: $COHR, $LITE, $AAOI, $FN, $POET, $MRVL, $CRDO Top performer: Lumentum (LITE) +1,685% Vertically integrated across L2 and L6A. Supply locked by hyperscalers. Pricing power persists. L6B: Infrastructure Fiber and cable. The physical nervous system of every AI cluster. Boring until hyperscalers start spending $50B+ on datacenter campuses. Then it’s anything but. Key names: $GLW, $OTCPK:SMOKY, $PRY, $APH, $FJKUF Top performer: Fujikura (5803.TSE) +748% Japanese fiber giant. Direct beneficiary of AI intra-datacenter cabling demand going parabolic. L6C: Transport Moves data between datacenters across metro and long-haul fiber. As AI clusters get larger and more distributed, inter-datacenter bandwidth demand compounds. This layer doesn’t slow down. Key names: $CIEN, $NOK, $CSCO Top performer: Ciena (CIEN) +777% WaveLogic coherent optics is the dominant long-haul platform in North America and Europe L7: Test & Qualification The final gatekeeper. Every photonic device passes through here before shipping. Burn-in, optical test, IC test, wafer test. Often overlooked. Often the highest-margin layer. text:
As of April 23, 2026

The AI Photonics and Optical Infrastructure sector is showing a sharply bifurcated tape today, with large-cap AI infrastructure names holding green while speculative small and micro-cap photonics names unwind hard, the top loser being $IQE down 20.53%. The move reflects a parabolic blow-off on the London line (IQE.L ran over 107% in the two weeks into April 17 before rolling over), with the OTC mirror IQEPF gapping down today on profit-taking and zero fresh fundamental news into the May 20 earnings print (https://t.co/udYbhT79LN).

The quality leadership is sending a clear signal: capital is rotating out of speculative substrate and early-stage PIC names into the picks-and-shovels plays with visible AI capex pull-through. $MRVL leads at +5.24% on the Amazon-Anthropic $100B deal tailwind layered on top of the Google custom AI chip talks and Nvidia's $2B NVLink Fusion investment, which together anchor Marvell as the default optical DSP and custom ASIC partner across every hyperscaler (https://t.co/2jwCYbswdK).

$MRVL: 5.24%
$VIAV: 4.15%
$TER: 4.10%
$CIEN: 3.38%
$KEYS: 2.23%

$XNDU: -16.98%
$IQE: -20.53%

1. Optical Transceivers & Active Networking
$CIEN, $GLW: The large-cap optical layer held up on AI fabric demand, with Ciena catching a bid as 800G and 1.6T buildouts continue into 2H26 and Corning benefiting from the optical cable pull-through.
$LITE, $COHR: Both names gave back modestly after a multi-week run, looking like position-trimming rather than thesis breakdown ahead of earnings season.
$AAOI: Down 7.82%, this is the sharpest move among the transceiver names and reads as profit-taking on the recent domestic-manufacturing tariff-exemption bid plus microcap beta to the broader sector unwind.

2. Compound Semiconductor Substrates & Foundries
$GFS, $TSEM: Foundries split, with GlobalFoundries up on SiPho and advanced node traction while Tower gave back some of its recent run on sector risk-off.
$AXTI, $IQE, $SLOIF, $SIVEF: This is where the damage concentrated. AXTI down 13.42%, IQE down 20.53%, Soitec down 6.97%, SiTime down 7.78%. InP substrate demand remains the bottleneck thesis intact, but the OTC liquidity and parabolic-unwind dynamics are doing the driving today, not any fundamental deterioration.
$BESIY: Flat on the day, advanced packaging and hybrid bonding exposure providing some ballast.

3. Early-Stage Photonic Materials & PICs
$POET, $LWLG, $XNDU: All three got hit hard (POET -8.22%, LWLG -10.29%, XNDU -16.98%) in what looks like a coordinated speculative unwind. Cramer's measured POET comments on CNBC this morning didn't help sentiment on the name, and the broader early-stage PIC basket tends to move as one when risk comes off. Thesis remains long-dated optionality, but the tape is punishing anything that ran too far too fast.

The move reflects a parabolic blow-off on the London line (IQE.L ran over 107% in the two weeks into April 17 before rolling over), with the OTC mirror IQEPF gapping down today on profit-taking and zero fresh fundamental news into the May 20 earnings print (https://t.co/udYbhT79LN).

The quality leadership is sending a clear signal: capital is rotating out of speculative substrate and early-stage PIC names into the picks-and-shovels plays with visible AI capex pull-through. $MRVL leads at +5.24% on the Amazon-Anthropic $100B deal tailwind layered on top of the Google custom AI chip talks and Nvidia's $2B NVLink Fusion investment, which together anchor Marvell as the default optical DSP and custom ASIC partner across every hyperscaler (https://t.co/2jwCYbswdK).

$AAOI: Down 7.82%, this is the sharpest move among the transceiver names and reads as profit-taking on the recent domestic-manufacturing tariff-exemption bid plus microcap beta to the broader sector unwind.

2. Compound Semiconductor Substrates & Foundries
$GFS, $TSEM: Foundries split, with GlobalFoundries up on SiPho and advanced node traction while Tower gave back some of its recent run on sector risk-off.
$AXTI, $IQE, $SLOIF, $SIVEF: This is where the damage concentrated. AXTI down 13.42%, IQE down 20.53%, Soitec down 6.97%, SiTime down 7.78%. InP substrate demand remains the bottleneck thesis intact, but the OTC liquidity and parabolic-unwind dynamics are doing the driving today, not any fundamental deterioration.
$BESIY: Flat on the day, advanced packaging and hybrid bonding exposure providing some ballast.

3. Early-Stage Photonic Materials & PICs
$POET, $LWLG, $XNDU: All three got hit hard (POET -8.22%, LWLG -10.29%, XNDU -16.98%) in what looks like a coordinated speculative unwind. user_name: Asteris - Socials on Autopilot with Your Content!
text:
Photonic computing for edge AI is one of those stories that sounds niche until you think about energy.

If NeuraWave delivers faster inference with lower power draw, that matters anywhere AI has to live outside giant data centres.

#EdgeAI #Photonics #AIHardware

Anders Storm’s repeated, specific praise is a strong and credible counter to blanket “pumper” accusations against Serenity (@aleabitoreddit). @stormdirac 

Storm is not a random retail account: He was CEO of Sivers for 8–10 years (built the photonics side). He knows the tech, partnerships (Ayar Labs, Jabil, etc.), and execution realities better than almost anyone outside the current management. 

He’s not hyping blindly - he engages with Serenity’s threads on supply-chain mapping and calls them “great deep research.”

text:
$QCOM is asymmetrical because it's being priced to fail.

The demand for CPUs is exploding and $ARM $INTC $AMD are the biggest winners.

But remember in February comparing $AAOI --> $LITE $COHR?  It was an underdog in photonics and then the market acknowledged it had the assets to be a credible 3rd place and deserved a higher MC.

$QCOM could be similar in that they have a credible set of assets to getting in on a different supercycle: CPUs.

This is intriguing because it trades at 12x forward PE. $ARM is 110x. $INTC is 125x. $AMD is 40x.

In 2021 Qualcomm bought Nuvia for $1.4B, the ex-Apple silicon team that designed the M-series cores which are the gold standard. At Computex 2025 they announced NVLink Fusion with Nvidia which means Qualcomm CPUs could slot into AI factories the same way Xeon does today.

On the Q3 FY25 call, Amon disclosed "advanced discussions with a leading hyperscaler" and guided data center revenue starting fiscal 2028. 

Three products are coming: a CPU, accelerators, and a full rack. The $2.4B Alphawave acquisition provides the high-speed connectivity IP to ship it.

Also in January 2025 they hired Sailesh Kottapalli, former chief architect of Intel Xeon.

Wednesday's earnings is the swing factor. Name the hyperscaler or guide data center revenue separately and there's some juice. 

At 12x forward, zero data center upside is priced in so there is clear potential for multiple expansion.

text:
NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang talked with Dwarkesh Patel that he believes that the way to grow AI is to continue to support the AI ecosystem across all facets.

"The investments that we've done with Lumentum and Coherent and all of the silicon photonics ecosystem, the last several years, we really reshaped the ecosystem and the supply chain of silicon photonics...

We're preparing the supply chain through invention of new technologies, new workflows, new testing equipment, double-sided probing, investing in companies, helping them scale up their capacity."

- CEO of @nvidia Jensen Huang on the @Dwarkesh Podcast

text:
The 7-Layer Photonics Stack

1.Materials & Wafers — InP, GaAs, silicon substrates. InP supply is the chokepoint.

https://t.co/X8pBSexGF9 & Epitaxy — Equipment to grow the crystal layers. MOCVD machines. Few players.

3.Lasers & Devices — VCSELs, DFB lasers, modulators. Where the photons actually get made.

4.Foundries (III-V Fabs) — The fabs that process compound semiconductors. Scarce and specialized.

5.Test & Inspection — Verify everything works at speed and scale. Lower binary risk, often overlooked.

6.Optics & Modules — Transceivers, MEMS, co-packaged optics (CPO). Where hyperscaler demand hits first.

7.Networking & Connectivity — Optical switches, DSPs, interconnect. The system layer.

text:
Ich habe die Liste geklaut, aber einfach um sicher zu gehen, dass auch in der deutschen Bubble ein paar Leute vom Thema #Photonic mitbekommen.

THE 7 LAYERS OF PHOTONICS

1. Materials & Wafers (substrate layer)
$AXTI, $IQE $WOLF, $COHR, $LWLG

2. Tools (fabrication layer)
$ASML, $AMAT, $LRCX 

3. Lasers (light generation layer)
$LITE, $COHR, $LASR, $SMTC 

4. Foundries (manufacturing layer)
$TSM, $TSEM, $GFS, $UMC, $INTC

5. Test, Inspection & Packaging (reliability layer)
$AEHR, $VIAV, $ONTO, $AMKR, $FN

6. Optics (module layer)
$AAOI, $POET, $GLW

7. Networking (connectivity layer)
$CRDO, $MRVL, $AVGO, $ANET, $CIEN

text:
c:7

$M7U (Nynomic AG) is a German micro-cap, off the beaten path, directly part of the photonics supply chain.

Here’s my simplified explanation of what this company does and why I like it:

AI datacenters need massive amounts of fiber optic connections to move data between chips at the speed of light. Those fiber optic components are manufactured inside specialized reactors made by a company called AIXTRON – which controls roughly 90% of the global market for these machines.

Nynomic owns LayTec – a company that makes the sensors that sit inside every single one of those AIXA reactors and measure what’s happening in real time during production. Without them, manufacturing yields drop dramatically. Near 1:1 attach rate on every reactor AIXA ships.

AIXA’s optics segment is their primary growth engine for 2026, guided at €520M revenue. LayTec rides that wave on every single order.

Now the valuation gap compared to its nearest competitors. $ONTO trades at ~12.7x sales. $CAMT trades at ~15x sales. Nynomic trades at ~1.5x sales. 

The market is currently treating it like a struggling industrial company because of the 2025 restructuring noise – but management guides for significant recovery in 2026 with disproportionately large profit expansion. Medium-term target is €200M revenue at 16-19% margins.

Given the current market cap, hitting those targets would likely force a massive re-rating toward the 3x–5x sales range typical for European specialized semi-cap players.

You’re essentially buying a hidden monopoly inside the hottest semiconductor supply chain in the world at a steep discount – before it has had a chance to run.

This is a position I opened on $IBKR last week. Not available on $HOOD. Looking to add more this week if the price dips or stays flat a little longer.

Here’s my simplified explanation of what this company does and why I like it: AI datacenters need massive amounts of fiber optic connections to move data between chips at the speed of light. Those fiber optic components are manufactured inside specialized reactors made by a company called AIXTRON – which controls roughly 90% of the global market for these machines. Nynomic owns LayTec – a company that makes the sensors that sit inside every single one of those AIXA reactors and measure what’s happening in real time during production. Medium-term target is €200M revenue at 16-19% margins. The thing I learned from bitcoin is that supply/demand imbalances don't get resolved overnight.

The same thing applies to AI.

There are shortages in GPUs, CPUs, data centers, power generation, photonics, chemicals, and much more.

These imbalances won't go away anytime soon.

text:
It's highly nuanced, and I'll explain why it's not late, but late to some: 

Photonics is the newest supercycle (maybe H1 into H2 2025 was the start).

Then there's many different architectural changes in each supercycle:

-> $LITE, $COHR, Innolight, $AXTI and these names led the first 

I did a thesis post on mentioning all four of them as the largest beneficiaries (all are up 500-1000% 1Y)

-> $AAOI, $JBL and others types of names are benefit immensely as the transitional bridge (eg. 1.6T pluggable) 

-> $SIVE, Celestial, Ayar, $POET are others future gens eg. CPO (what I'm focusing on now)

-> VisEra, QD Laser, $ALMU and others are likely going to be future gens (quantum dot, different packaging types, etc) if you fast forward 4 years. 

Of course, $LITE does everything. $AXTI will be used for everything. 

But the amount of pure play exposure for each architectural shift in each mini supercycle is different. 

For example, inp usage with quantum dot is still there, but less used. Or DFB laser arrays for CPO instead of EML. 

There's probably still 50%+ with $LITE and $COHR. And you're a little on the "late" side of things. 

But you're extremely early to new architecture generations. 

What I'm trying to do is point regular retail investors into the direction of new gold mines for free. 

Before institutions figure out sooner or later by paying $20k for equity research reports.

text:
Copper Wall Account Update 🚀

Launched this account last Monday (4/20) — already +4% in under a week.
$5/week DCA into my AI/photonics basket:

$MRVL (data center semis & networking)
$AVGO (AI networking & semiconductors)
$FN (optical component manufacturing)
$CRDO (high-speed connectivity for AI)
$TSEM (specialty analog foundry)
$ARM (CPU architecture powering AI chips)
$ALAB (AI/cloud connectivity solutions)
$TSM (world’s leading chip foundry)
$LITE (optical & photonic components)
$AAOI (fiber-optic networking products)
$HON (industrial tech & automation)
$IONQ (trapped-ion quantum computing)

Thesis: Photonics could be the next major unlock in data processing, and we’re only in Year 3 of a 10-year AI buildout.

This account should look strong by 2032. 

Bullish? 💡📈

text:
THE PHOTONICS ROTATION

Almost nobody is watching photonics.

As AI clusters scale, copper hits physical limits and the next bottleneck becomes optical infrastructure.

Here are 15 names positioned for it:

1. $LITE owns the laser + optical switching side of the trade and is one of the cleanest pure plays on AI optical demand.

2. $COHR wins from lasers, modules, and networking hardware that power hyperscale AI infrastructure and cloud expansion.

3. $AAOI is one of the best ways to play AI optical transceivers with major hyperscaler demand for 800G and 1.6T connectivity.

4. $SIVE benefits from the push toward faster semiconductor-to-optical integration as AI infrastructure scales.

5. $MRVL controls a huge part of the DSP + interconnect story with optical networking chips and high-speed connectivity.

6. $AVGO sits at the center of AI networking through switching, custom silicon, and optical interconnect demand.

7. $ANET is the Ethernet backbone moving massive AI workloads across hyperscale clusters and data centers.

8. $GLW supplies the specialty glass + fiber needed for the optical transport layer behind AI infrastructure.

9. $JBL benefits from building and scaling the actual hardware behind networking systems and optical modules.

10. $AEHR wins from burn-in + testing demand as AI ASICs and high-power optical hardware move into production.

11. $POET is focused on lower-cost optical engines designed to improve efficiency inside AI data centers.

12. $LWLG is pushing next-gen polymer photonics that could make optical communication faster and more efficient.

13. $QCLS brings exposure to advanced laser systems supporting precision photonics and next-gen optical demand.

14. $LPTH provides specialty optics and photonic components tied to industrial, defense, and AI-driven systems.

15. $ALAB gives exposure to the connectivity + infrastructure side helping AI clusters scale faster.

Most people won’t care until these are already up 100%.

Here are 15 names positioned for it: 1. $LITE owns the laser + optical switching side of the trade and is one of the cleanest pure plays on AI optical demand. 2. $COHR wins from lasers, modules, and networking hardware that power hyperscale AI infrastructure and cloud expansion. 3. $AAOI is one of the best ways to play AI optical transceivers with major hyperscaler demand for 800G and 1.6T connectivity. 4. $SIVE benefits from the push toward faster semiconductor-to-optical integration as AI infrastructure scales. 5. $MRVL controls a huge part of the DSP + interconnect story with optical networking chips and high-speed connectivity. 6. $AVGO sits at the center of AI networking through switching, custom silicon, and optical interconnect demand. 7. $ANET is the Ethernet backbone moving massive AI workloads across hyperscale clusters and data centers. 8. $GLW supplies the specialty glass + fiber needed for the optical transport layer behind AI infrastructure. 9. $JBL benefits from building and scaling the actual hardware behind networking systems and optical modules. 10. $AEHR wins from burn-in + testing demand as AI ASICs and high-power optical hardware move into production. 11. $POET is focused on lower-cost optical engines designed to improve efficiency inside AI data centers. 12. $LWLG is pushing next-gen polymer photonics that could make optical communication faster and more efficient. 13. $QCLS brings exposure to advanced laser systems supporting precision photonics and next-gen optical demand. 14. $LPTH provides specialty optics and photonic components tied to industrial, defense, and AI-driven systems. 15. $ALAB gives exposure to the connectivity + infrastructure side helping AI clusters scale faster. text:
The recent strong rally in Credo Technology (CRDO) was sparked by its April 14 announcement acquiring DustPhotonics for ~$1.2B (cash + stock + earnouts). This adds silicon photonics tech for 800G/1.6T+ optical transceivers, expanding beyond active electrical cables into faster AI data center interconnects—targeting $500M+ optical revenue by FY27.

It built on Jefferies' fresh Buy rating, explosive AI-driven growth (200%+ YoY revenue), and bullish order flow signals. Stock up ~46% in the past 5 days as of April 23.

@Thomas_james_1 $LITE near $850 vs $SIVE at $3 — valuation divergence in photonics: fundamentals or market cap effects? Small-cap photonics names offer higher beta in upcycles but with higher volatility — option spreads may work better than outright shares.

text:
Leeway AI: STM pivots to data centers while automotive stalls and politics cloud the room
$STM

STMicroelectronics started mass production of silicon photonics chips for AI data centers in Q1 2026 and expanded its 800-volt DC power portfolio alongside NVIDIA, while deepening ties with AWS to strengthen its position in AI infrastructure.

The company completed its acquisition of NXP's MEMS business, expected to contribute mid-range revenue in the $40 million area over time, with particular strength in automotive and industrial sensors.

In China, STM began series production of its STM32 H7 microcontroller family, with plans to extend to H5 and C5 variants, improving supply chain resilience against geopolitical pressure.The momentum came against headwinds in Q4 2025. A net loss of $30 million, impairment charges and restructuring costs totaling $141 million, and a 31.6% revenue decline in the power and discrete segment weighed heavily on results.

Management is budgeting $2 to $2.2 billion in capital expenditure for 2026, mostly directed toward SiC and 300-millimeter capacity expansion.The company sees normalization of customer inventory levels by the end of Q2 2026 as the critical threshold for sustainable recovery.

text:
The photonics bottleneck had SO many layers.

That's what it made it so damn fun.

But I think it's messed with our heads a little on FinX.

We got a bit addicted to hunting niche small cap upstream plays because there were many.

But when it comes to insane implications of the CPU bottleneck, feels like it's more about just owning the leaders.

Tell me why $INTC $ARM $AMD can't be $1T companies when GPU:CPU ratio shifts from 8:1 to 1:1. 

Literally an 8x of the TAM on what was already a $5T buildout?

"richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2439}, {"from_index": 2465, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2499}]}, "text": "What makes a company truly compelling is not just what it is today, but how many powerful trends it could end up riding at once. That is a big part of why $SIVE / $SIVEF stands out to me. Most investors build exposure one theme at a time. They buy one company for AI, another for defense, another for satcom, another for photonics, and maybe yet another for next-generation telecom. It is a bit like assembling the future one puzzle piece at a time, hoping the picture eventually comes together. Sivers feels different. It feels more like finding one piece that already connects several of the most important parts of that picture. At first glance, it is easy to label it as just another semiconductor company. But the more I look at it, the more that feels like an oversimplification. Sivers sits in the overlap between mmWave, photonics, datacenter connectivity, satcom and defense. Those are not niche or isolated markets. They are major long-term themes, and the company has exposure to several of them at once. That is what makes the setup so compelling. Sivers does not need every single door to open at the same time for the story to work. It may be enough for one or two of these markets to accelerate, one or two partnerships to deepen, or one part of the business to gain meaningful commercial traction before investors begin to see the company in a very different light. That is why I think the “one stock, several themes” angle matters here. For a new investor, Sivers may be easier to understand if you stop thinking about it as a single narrow bet and start thinking about it as a small platform with multiple shots on goal. Not one straight road, but a crossroads. Not one engine, but several potential drivers of value that could begin contributing at different times. Of course, that does not remove the risk. Stories with several moving parts are never simple. But they can also create exactly the kind of asymmetry that makes small-cap investing so interesting, especially when the market has not fully decided how to categorize the company yet. And that is often where the upside starts to emerge. So the appeal of Sivers, to me, is not just that it has exposure to attractive end markets. It is that you do not need to buy five different companies to get that exposure. You are looking at one company that could potentially benefit from several major technology shifts at once. That kind of setup is rare. And that is why I think Sivers deserves a much closer look."}}}, "rest_id": "2045914937076183188", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "35291", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2048514663634108784", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "211", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Bottlenecks are the theme of 2026.

We’ve already had:

→ memory bottlenecks
→ photonics bottlenecks
→ CPU bottlenecks
→ GPU bottlenecks

But the next question is bigger:
What powers it all?

AI data centres don’t just need chips.
They need electricity at insane scale.

My power watchlist:

→ $CEG for nuclear
→ $VST for power generation
→ $TLN for nuclear/data centre deals
→ $GEV for turbines + grid equipment
→ $ETN for electrical infrastructure
→ $PWR for grid buildout
→ $OKLO / $SMR for SMRs

The next AI bottleneck will not be inside the data centre.
It will be the power needed to run it.

text:
$LWLG

Don’t miss this one. A pullback soon back could happen. 

This looks a lot like buying $COHR before photonics became a core AI data center bottleneck theme.

AI photonics new age is here. If this sticks, this one could be huge. 

Revenue growth Q: +594%
Gross profit growth Q: +619%
~$69M current assets
~$66M net cash position
~8450 PS ratio 

(reflects extremely early revenue base, not mature scaling)

Still in commercialization / validation phase for EO polymer photonics

This is like buying $COHR at 15$ in 2014

All at a 2B market cap. 

We saw it with $LITE and $COHR — optical infrastructure compounds hard once AI data center demand shifts from narrative to structural bottleneck.

That’s the same structural lane where photonics becomes the invisible layer of compute scaling.

Not saying $LWLG becomes $COHR.

This is where those asymmetries live.

text:
First time I see someone on my feed recommending $AIP. It's nice to see different stocks than the traditional photonics / 800G plays that everybody hypes every day.

I'm also quite bullish on $AIP, although I sold my shares for a quick profit. What I really like is that they are actively decelerating their China business. It accounted for 25-30% before, which was a massive geopolitical headwind.

The downside for me is the royalty timeline. Those revenues only materialize a couple of years after the licensing deal is signed. So while I really like the stock, I'm not sure if it is the best timing to hold right now, but we'll see. The market reflects future potential, not current value so very possible it could go higher in the near term propulsed by INTC and AMD

text:
Jensen's 5-Layer AI framework is the cleanest investment thesis you'll see in 2026. Energy. Compute. Photonics. Memory. Chips. Every layer is in shortage. Every layer is repricing higher.

Layer 1: Energy. AI training campuses pulling 1+ gigawatts each. Data center power demand growing 40 percent annually. Bloom Energy, Oklo, Vertiv. Bull thesis is small modular reactors and fuel cells get the AI buildout contracts. Bear thesis is grid backlogs delay the buildout 3 years.

Layer 2: Compute. Hyperscaler GPU clusters can't keep up. CoreWeave, Nebius, IREN, Cipher. The neoclouds capture overflow demand at premium pricing. Bull case is 5x revenue. Bear case is hyperscalers self-supply and squeeze them out.

Layer 3: Photonics. Networking inside data centers is the new bottleneck. Applied Optoelectronics, Lumentum, Coherent, Astera Labs. 800G and 1.6T optical transceivers sold out through 2027. Bull case is networking captures real margin. Bear case is hyperscalers vertically integrate optics.

Layer 4: Memory. The bottleneck inside the bottleneck. Micron, SanDisk, Seagate, plus SK Hynix and Samsung internationally. DA Davidson just put a $1,000 PT on Micron. Bull case is structurally extended cycle. Bear case is China NAND oversupply.

Layer 5: Chips. NVDA, AMD, Broadcom, plus custom silicon at Google, Amazon, Meta. Bull case is NVDA's CUDA moat holds 5 more years. Bear case is custom silicon takes 30 percent share by 2028 and the stack price-compresses.

What nobody's saying: this isn't a "buy everything AI" framework.

It's a structural map of where shortages compound. Every layer has supply tight through 2027. Pick the layer with the deepest moat and the cheapest valuation.

My answer: memory at Layer 4. Energy at Layer 1.

The map is real.

Pick your shovels.

today: 
"wins a $21M Texas grant to expand semiconductor and photonics manufacturing in Sugar Land.  The project adds a 210K sq. ft. facility for AI data center transceiver production and is expected to create 500+ jobs." https://t.co/wKWAy76O36

Foxconn Is Ready to Embrace CPO, as Silicon Photonic Products Manage to Reach 3.2T Transmission Capacity 

On the 29th, Guo Haozhong, director of the Foxconn Semiconductor Research Institute, delivered a speech on silicon photonics trends at the 2026 MIC FORUM Spring "Intelligent New Order" seminar. He said that Foxconn's (2317) silicon photonics products can already achieve a transmission capacity of 3.2T, but with the continued shortage of InP capacity, silicon photonics will still face 10 challenges to overcome in the future.

This year, the slogan for AI servers has changed from "fiber to copper" to "fiber and copper in parallel". This is mainly because Broadcom chips have optimized the 200G transmission speed. However, copper wires will become increasingly difficult to use, especially high-speed copper wires which are heavy and require external sheathing. The advantage of optical communication is that a single wire can carry multiple wavelengths simultaneously without interfering with each other, up to a maximum of eight wavelengths.

Guo Haozhong stated that Foxconn's modulator can already achieve a single-channel transmission speed of 200G, using 2 or 4 different wavelength lasers and employing WDM (wavelength division multiplexing) technology to integrate on a PIC (photonic integrated circuit) chip; for a single-channel 200G, if two groups of four wavelengths are used, a transmission capacity of 3.2T can be achieved, and it even has the ability to upgrade the technology to 6.4T in the future.
Since future data center transmission speeds will reach over 400G, he stated that the current single-channel transmission rate of silicon photonics is limited to nearly 200G and has high power requirements. InP has advantages in high-speed transmission, not only integrating lasers but also helping to support transmission of systems above 400G. However, InP material is not as stable as silicon, resulting in low yield and high cost. Currently, due to insufficient production capacity, many companies are also investing in the research and development of other materials, such as TFLN or SOH, which have become materials of interest.

InP has advantages in high-speed transmission, not only integrating lasers but also helping to support transmission of systems above 400G. {"from_index": 2228, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2257}, {"from_index": 2423, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2467}]}, "text": "I think one of the most underestimated parts of the Sivers / $SIVE story is the SATCOM opportunity with https://t.co/A7mlfHMHLp. This is not just another customer relationship. Sivers has been working with https://t.co/A7mlfHMHLp for years through several development programs worth many millions of dollars, and the relationship appears to go much deeper than a normal supplier agreement. Sivers is part of the technology roadmap for advanced satellite terminals that are built for a world where connectivity will move across different orbits, networks and operators. That is where the potential becomes interesting. According to $SIVE own material, a SATCOM terminal can contain up to around 1,800 Sivers chips, with potential Sivers value of up to around $10,000 per terminal. That changes the way you should think about scale. This is not a consumer hardware story where millions of units are needed before revenue matters. In this case, even relatively small deployment numbers can become meaningful very quickly. If https://t.co/A7mlfHMHLp sells 1,000 terminals, that is up to around $10 million for Sivers. At 5,000 terminals, up to around $50 million. At 10,000 terminals, up to around $100 million. And that is from one customer platform. This is why I think the market may be underestimating SATCOM. The focus is often on when Wireless can become meaningful again, but the answer may not only come from huge 5G volumes. It could also come from a smaller number of high-value satellite terminals where Sivers has very high value per terminal. The timing also matters. $SIVE has indicated that volume ramp is expected in H2 2026. If that happens, SATCOM moves from development revenue into something much more important: recurring product revenue tied to actual terminal deployments. And the backdrop is strong. https://t.co/A7mlfHMHLp is already working with demanding customers and partners such as the US Army, NATO-related defence programs, Viasat, SES and Intelsat, while defence demand for resilient satellite communication is increasing and commercial networks are moving toward more advanced multi-orbit connectivity. Terminals need to become smarter, more capable and more complex. That is exactly where Sivers beamforming technology becomes valuable. For me, this is one of the most interesting parts of the $SIVE story into 2026. Not because it requires massive volumes. But because in SATCOM, small volumes can already become big numbers. #SATCOM #Beamforming #SatelliteCommunications #SpaceTech #DefenceTech #MultiOrbit #SIVEF"}}}, "rest_id": "2049351404595163589", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "12990", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2049540957603831884", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "604", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Laser Photonics $LASE, yaklaşık 5.7 milyon adet varantın $0.70 fiyatla kullanılmasıyla ~4 milyon dolar nakit elde etti. 

Bunun karşılığında yatırımcılara, $0.975 kullanım fiyatlı yeni varantlar (Series A-5 ve A-6) verildi.

 Bu yeni varantlar hissedar onayı sonrası devreye girecek ve 2–5 yıl vadeli olacak. 

İşlem kısa vadede nakit sağlarken, ileride hisse sulandırma riski yaratıyor.

text:
Arrogance is costly.

Some of the highly regarded Korean photonics crew are about to get punished… because they’re still looking at charts on a 15-minute / yearly basis.

https://t.co/IIoZNOe7gU

“Price is king” is a lesson they still need to learn.
Warned this guy earlier (tweet below).
But we all learn at our own pace.

The real lesson: solid analyst teams and influencers with great picks, but limited experience pumping overbought stocks to their followers right before a 40–50% correction.

$IQE is headed to the 23–28 range.
We’ll be buyers then.

text:
We will be taking a short position in Semis here.
Short trade expected to last a couple of weeks. After that, we’ll look to pick up bottoms in select photonics names following a solid correction/profit-taking phase.

Trade setup:
- Using SMH, SPXL, and SOXS
- Earnings pop into mega tech earnings today
- Ideal entry on SOXS in the $13.60–$14.25 range
- Targeting 25–30% return over the next 2 weeks

Some photonic stocks will likely sell off hard (some already started) - which should create a good buying opportunity for those who missed the big rally.

Stay tuned.

text:
$TYGO closed Fri at $5.01 (+2.45%), after-hours $5.18. Q1 earnings May 5, fresh product launches in solar/storage, analysts targeting ~$4.50-$6.

$POET exploded +28.84% to $15.10 on new orders (11-yr high), huge volume. After-hours up more. AI photonics momentum strong but volatile.

Short-term direction looks up on catalysts for both. NFA.

Sivers’ photonics division manufactures semiconductor-based optical products, including III-V compound semiconductor laser devices for LiDAR and Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) in AI data centers. They recently partnered with companies like Jabil and O-Net Technologies to supply high-performance laser arrays that help move massive amounts of data using light instead of electricity. However, they face significant competition in the optical networking and CPO space.

Written by Grok. Sivers Semiconductors AB (STO: SIVE / OTC: SIVEF) is a Swedish company specializing in advanced semiconductor technologies, with two main segments: Wireless (mmWave for 5G/6G, SATCOM, defense) and Photonics (III-V compound semiconductor lasers and arrays).0 The Photonics business is driving recent excitement, particularly in AI data centers. Core Technology for AI Sivers develops high-power Distributed Feedback (DFB) laser arrays — continuous-wave (CW) lasers that serve as light sources for high-speed optical interconnects. These are critical for moving data with light (photons) instead of electricity inside AI clusters, addressing power, heat, latency, and bandwidth bottlenecks as GPU/accelerator counts explode.28 Key advantages include: •High output power (>50mW per channel) •Wavelength stability for CWDM/DWDM •Reliability for data center environments These lasers support pluggable transceivers (e.g., 1.6T modules) and emerging co-packaged optics (CPO) / external light sources.20 gUlWK“LARGE” jUwE4“LARGE” dBwjt“LARGE” Key Partnerships & Momentum •Jabil collaboration (April 2026): Developing energy-efficient 1.6T linear receive optical (LRO) transceiver modules using Sivers lasers — targeting hyperscale AI data centers with ~2.5x lower energy footprint.20 •POET Technologies, Ayar Labs (SuperNova), O-Net, Enablence, and others for CPO/external light sources.22 •Manufacturing scale-up with WIN Semiconductors for high-volume DFB production. •Early revenue from AI/photonics customers (e.g., Ayar Labs order) plus a growing pipeline ($453M non-binding opportunities reported).45 LiDAR (autonomous/sensing) provides additional diversification and near-term revenue ramp.23 5WgcU“LARGE” Hsdff“LARGE” Why It Could Be a “Next Big Winner” in AI (High-Risk/High-Reward Thesis) AI training/inference demands ever-higher bandwidth and efficiency. Copper interconnects hit physical limits; optics (photonics) are the logical next step for intra-rack and chip-to-chip links. Sivers sits in the “picks and shovels” layer — supplying the lasers that power the optical plumbing.40 If CPO and external laser architectures gain traction (as pushed by players tied to NVIDIA/Marvell ecosystems), the addressable market for these components could be substantial. Analysts and investors model scenarios where Sivers captures a slice leading to $100M+ annual revenue by 2028 in optimistic cases, at attractive multiples for a growth story.49 The stock has already rerated sharply (hundreds of percent in the past year as of April 2026) on this narrative, now trading at a ~$900M–$1B+ market cap range depending on share price.42 Risks are real: Still pre-scale in high-volume photonics, execution on conversions from sampling to production, competition, cash burn, and dilution potential. It’s a speculative small-cap play — not guaranteed, but one of the more credible pure-plays in AI photonics. Bottom line: Sivers is a specialized enabler in the shift from electrons to photons in AI infrastructure. With real tech, ecosystem ties, and commercialization steps underway, it offers asymmetric upside if AI optics scales as expected.46 HE6AR“LARGE” EIkCL“LARGE” For the latest financials, check their investor site or recent earnings. text:
Three very different bets on the same frontier are drawing investor attention right now: trapped-ion systems delivered through major cloud platforms, quantum annealing with hybrid cloud solvers, and integrated photonics devices that operate at room temperature.

What makes this moment fascinating is the architectural diversity. The market is not converging on a single approach. It is branching out, with each pathway targeting different problem sets and deployment models.

Cloud-accessible superconducting and trapped-ion machines serve complex research and optimization workloads. Annealing systems carve out niches in logistics and materials science. Photonic approaches promise lower infrastructure overhead and novel applications in authentication and random number generation.

For businesses watching from the sidelines, the practical takeaway is that quantum is not a monolith. The technology you eventually adopt will depend on your specific use cases, latency requirements, and integration constraints, not on which stock ticker had the best week.

We are still in the infrastructure-building phase. Companies laying the groundwork today in R&D, partnerships, and IP portfolios are making long-horizon bets that commercial adoption will reward patience over hype.

Understanding what each approach actually does, and where its limitations lie, is the difference between an informed strategy and expensive guesswork #QuantumComputing

text:
Optical communication is rapidly evolving, enabling faster and more efficient global data transmission. Innovations in photonics, fibre optics, and wavelength multiplexing are driving unprecedented bandwidth and reliability. Engineers at Hamamatsu Photonics are advancing high-sensitivity photodiodes and semiconductor laser technologies that underpin modern telecom and data centre infrastructure. These breakthroughs reduce signal loss, increase speed, and support terabit-scale networks across submarine cables and cloud systems. 

As global demand for real-time data grows, optical systems are redefining connectivity with higher efficiency and lower energy use. 

Learn more: https://t.co/osOJovJNha

#engineering #technology #optics #photonics 

@HamamatsuPhoton

text:
The Call for Presentations is still open for the 2026 OCP APAC Summit, taking place August 11 to 12 in Taiwan.

From AI infrastructure and advanced packaging to cooling, photonics, and open data centers, this is where the industry comes together to share what’s next.

This year’s program spans 11 key areas, including AI Clusters, Networking, Photonics, Chiplets & Advanced Packaging, and more.

Submit your proposal by May 18 and be part of the conversations shaping the future of open compute.

Learn more: https://t.co/lihu2mnu9k Registration is now open.

Thank you to our Selection Committee, which includes leaders from across the ecosystem who will guide speaker and content selection.

@OpenComputePrj

text:
Private AI portfolios betting on photonics, while retail investors are scrambling to get in. 

Meanwhile, Gilbert Cisneros - on the House Armed Services: Intelligence and Special Operations subcommittee - quietly bought Coherent Corp in March. 

That's the photonics company making laser components for directed energy systems. Different kind of conviction.

text:
i keep getting $AXTI vibes from it.

What does $POET tech do?
Photonics is nextgen.  $AXTI proves this.  Placing the laser is labor intensive/expensive/time consuming manual process.  $POET integrates photonics into a chip module.

WHY BUY?
1) needed for next gen AI hardware.
$AMZN - AI chips hitting ceiling - $POET solves this
$MSFT - CHIP production wall - $POET solves this
$META - favors Open Compute standards.  Arista is leader in this and works w/ $POET.  $META + $POET partnership inevitable

$TSLA - investing in DataCenter + Chips.  Will need $POET to get to next gen status

$AVGO & $NVDA - logical that one will buy $POET to prevent competitors from having nextgen bottleneck.

2) low fear dilution - cash runway 8 years

3) moat - 60 patents - years for competitor to replicate

4) no competitor

Tidbit
$AXTI got 4 straight days of gap ups.  UNICORN RARE.  $POET got 3 straight days.
